title: Composition Boundaries
use_for: defining where hosts, modules, and services compose
making integration seams explicit between runtime areas
separating composition ownership from feature implementation
keeping composition reviewable across larger systems
do_not_use_for: raw plugin API shape without composition concerns
rules: define composition seams before spreading registration logic
treat composition ownership as a boundary concern, not a feature-local convenience
prefer one clear integration surface per module relationship
separate contract ownership from wiring ownership
procedure: Identify the runtime areas that must compose.
Define the seam where each area may depend on another.
Assign which side owns contracts, which side owns wiring, and which side owns lifecycle entry.
Specify how modules join the host without leaking internal wiring concerns.
Verify composition remains inspectable at the seam.
output: explicit ownership of contracts, wiring, and lifecycle entry

primary_when: the request is specifically about module boundaries, host-module composition, or integration seams between runtime areas
the request is about who owns contracts versus who owns wiring across larger runtime boundaries
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about one local object graph, one feature implementation, or pure extension hook shape

solves: how to define explicit seams where runtime areas compose
how to stop composition logic from dissolving into global registration sprawl
how to keep module integration inspectable across a larger host
does_not_solve: fine-grained feature logic inside one module

core_principles: Composition boundaries should name where systems meet, not just what types exist.
Contracts, wiring, and lifecycle entry do not always belong to the same side of a seam.
Large-system composition should stay explicit enough to review without tracing hidden registrations.
constraints: A composition seam must remain visible in code and policy, not inferred from side effects.
Modules should not acquire host internals just because wiring is convenient.
Composition spread across many unrelated files should be pulled back to an explicit seam.
decision_rules: Use composition-boundaries as the owner when the main design question is where integration between runtime areas should occur.
If the seam also exposes extensibility to third parties, attach extension-points after the composition seam is clear.
If the seam's main concern is container registration, attach DI only after boundary ownership is defined.
If a module can only integrate by reaching through host internals, redesign the seam.

preferred_patterns: clear ownership split between contracts and wiring
module integration through named boundaries instead of ambient container access
composition root awareness at major runtime seams
avoid_patterns: global registration sprawl across unrelated modules
module code reaching into host setup internals
composition seams implied only by naming conventions
container access used as a substitute for real boundaries

recipes.name: Define a host-module composition seam
recipes.when: Use when a larger runtime must integrate modules without dissolving into hidden wiring.
steps: Name the host side, module side, and shared contract surface.
Define which side owns registration and lifecycle entry.
Limit integration to a small explicit seam.
Verify consumers do not need host setup internals to participate.

pitfalls: Treating module composition as a byproduct of container access.
Letting registration logic spread until no real seam remains.
Mixing contract ownership, wiring ownership, and feature behavior in one place.
review_checklist: Is ownership split clearly between contracts, wiring, and lifecycle entry?
Can modules integrate without reaching through host internals?
Is composition still reviewable without tracing hidden registrations?

references.title: Local Reference: Composition Boundaries
references.interpretation: Use this first for host-module seams, ownership splits, and explicit integration boundaries.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for larger-system composition structure.

references.title: Local Reference: Framework Contracts
references.interpretation: Use this to keep composition seams aligned with narrow contract ownership rather than container reach-through.
references.why: Use to align seam design with the framework's contract discipline.
